macro_segments.title: Trustworthiness of a Kohen's Testimony Regarding the Source of a Blemish: Transmission of Teaching from Gidul and Alternative Explanations

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Discussion on Trusting a Kohen's Claim About a Blemish from Yisrael
Rabbi Yirmiyahu bar Abba's Account of How Rabbi Yehuda's Teaching Was Originally Transmitted Through Gidul
The Case of a Growing Animal: Why a Yisrael Can Testify That He Gave the Bechor with a Blemish
Sura and Pumbedita Traditions: Different Interpretations of Whether a Kohen Can Be Believed About How He Received the Bechor
The Halakha Rules According to the Pumbedita Tradition: A Kohen Is Believed If the Matter Can Be Checked

macro_segments.title: The Story of Rafram and the Audacious Kohen: Testing the Limits of Trust When a Matter Can Be Revealed

micro_segments.title: Rafram in Pumbedita Gives a Kosher Bechor to a Kohen Without a Blemish
The Kohen Artificially Creates a Blemish and Attempts Deception When Rafram's Eyesight Is Weak
Rafram Recognizes the Animal Despite His Weak Eyes and Exposes the Kohen's Lie
Rafram Was Not Concerned by This Exception: The Brazen Kohen Did Not Invalidate the General Rule

macro_segments.title: The Case of an Obvious Blemish Brought Before Rav Ashi: The Requirement for Expert Confirmation Even When Fraud Is Unlikely

micro_segments.title: An Animal with an Obviously Permanent Blemish Comes Before Rav Ashi; No Concern Needed Regarding Its Source
Ravina's Challenge: What If the Owner Is a Yisrael, Not a Kohen? Rav Yehuda's Rule That a Yisrael Cannot Receive Ruling Without a Kohen Present
Why the Yisrael Would Not Hide the Animal: He Publicly Showed It to the Sage and Respected the Law
Conclusion: A Yisrael's Respect for the Sage's Ruling Demonstrates He Will Not Steal the Animal

macro_segments.title: Trusting Everyone Regarding Blemishes in Ma'aser: Why the Logic Differs From Bechor

micro_segments.title: The Principle: Everyone Is Trusted About Blemishes in Ma'aser Unlike in Bechor
Why Ma'aser Is Different: One Could Have Artificially Created a Blemish Before the Tenth Animal Was Selected

macro_segments.title: The Requirement for Expert or Beit Din Approval of a Blemish: Obvious Versus Non-Obvious Cases

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: Obviously Blemished Animals Require Ruling by Three Laypeople; Rabbi Yosi Disagrees and Always Requires an Expert
Gemara's Derivation: Three Laypeople Suffice Only for Obvious Blemishes Due to Rabbi Yosi's Exception Teaching
Rav Simlai and Rav Yehuda Nesi'ah in the Name of Rav Yehoshua ben Levi: Three People Can Release a Bechor Outside the Land Only If the Blemish Is Obvious
Rav's Clarification That Even Obvious Blemishes in Chutz La'aretz Require a Beit Din, Not an Expert
The Requirement That If an Expert Is Available, One Must Go to the Expert, Not Use a Beit Din

macro_segments.title: Three Laypeople Can Release a Bechor or Allow a Neder Only When No Expert Is Available; The Dispute With Rabbi Yosi and Rabbi Yehuda

micro_segments.title: Rav Chiya bar Amram: Three People Can Release a Bechor When No Expert Is Available, Excluding Rabbi Yosi's Position
Similarly, Three People Can Release a Neder When No Sage Is Available, Excluding Rabbi Yehuda's Requirement of a Sage
Understanding Rabbi Yehuda's Position: One of the Three Must Be a Sage for a Neder Release
Clarification of the Gemara: The Gemara Questions the Logic and Explores What Type of Sage Is Needed
Tosafot's Resolution: A Mid-Level Sage Is Required, One Capable of Explaining but Not Qualified to Release a Neder Alone
Rav Chananel and the Final Halakha: We Do Not Rule Like Rabbi Yosi; Three Laypeople Suffice If No Expert Is Available
The Gemara's Question About Potentially Redundant Statements from Rav or Shmuel Regarding Using a Beit Din Without an Expert

macro_segments.title: Selling a Bechor Without Expert Approval or a Treifah Animal: The Buyer's Remedies and Reimbursement Rules

micro_segments.title: One Who Slaughtered a Bechor Without Showing It to an Expert: The Meat Is Forbidden and Buyers Must Be Reimbursed
The Case of Selling a Treifah Animal: What the Buyers Ate Is Theirs to Keep; What Remains Must Be Returned or Buried
If Buyers Gave the Treifah Meat to Non-Jews or Dogs: They Receive Only a Discount, Not Full Reimbursement

macro_segments.title: Partial Reimbursement Based on Disgust: When Buyers Are Entitled to Full or Discounted Refunds

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon ben Elazar: Things That Cause Disgust Upon Discovery Warrant Full Reimbursement
Gemara's Examples: Neveila, Treifah, and Sheratzim Cause Disgust; Bechorot, Tevel, and Yayin Nesech Do Not
The Paradox: These Items Are Assur Be'hana'ah, So How Can the Seller Claim Any Benefit or Partial Payment?
Bechor's Exception: The Seller Lost the Opportunity to Get a Sage's Ruling After Slaughter, Justifying Partial Payment
Tevel's Solution: The Buyer Could Have Separated Terumot and Ma'asrot, So the Seller Lost Opportunity Cost
Yayin Nesech and Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel: Mixing with Permitted Wine Allows Partial Sale to Non-Jews, Justifying Partial Payment

macro_segments.title: The Definition and Parameters of Blemishes in a Bechor: Which Defects Are Permanent and Visible Enough to Qualify

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Following Blemishes Allow Slaughter of a Bechor; Mishnah Lists Ear Damage, Cracks, Holes, and Drying Out
Ear Damage: Damage to the Cartilage Is a Blemish, but Damage Only to the Skin Flap Is Not
Cracked Ears and Holes: Even Without Missing Parts, Cracks and Holes the Size of a Lentil Qualify
Dried-Out Ears: Defined as So Dry That Pricking It Produces No Blood; Rabbi Yosi Ben Meshullam Requires Even Greater Desiccation
Parallels to Lulav Defects: Similar Categories of Blemish Language Appear in the Laws of Lulav and Etrog

macro_segments.title: Hermeneutical Derivation of the Blemish Law: From 'Blind or Lame' to 'Any Bad Blemish' Using Rules of General, Specific, and General

micro_segments.title: The Torah Specifies 'Blind or Lame' but Also Says 'Any Bad Blemish': How Do We Generalize?
General-Specific-General Rule: We Generalize Only According to the Pattern of the Specific Examples Given
The Pattern of Blindness and Lameness: External, Visible, and Permanent; Only Similar Blemishes Qualify
Alternative Interpretation: A Blemish Must Be Visible and Must Impair the Animal's Usability (Melacha)
The Gemara's Objection: If Functionality Were Required, Why Would Ear Damage (Aesthetic Only) Qualify as a Blemish?
Resolution: 'Any Bad Blemish' Is a Ribui (Expansion) Showing That Aesthetic Defects Count, Not Just Functional Impairment
Hidden Blemishes Are Excluded: Why Tooth Damage Standards Differ—Molars Must Be Completely Missing Because They Cannot Be Seen
Conclusion: A 'Bad Blemish' Must Be Visible and Permanent; Non-Visible or Transitory Blemishes Do Not Qualify
Why Transitory Blemishes Are Excluded: They Cannot Even Redeem Other Sacrifices, So They Cannot Release a Bechor
